explicit_text: I was born in Sweden in a small town of about 3,000 people. My family had deep roots there; my father was a plant breeder and my mother an elementary school teacher. Growing up, I experienced the political and social changes in Sweden, like the introduction of universal healthcare when I was around 18 or 19. My schooling was also in Sweden, where I attended gymnasium and later taught math and science before immigrating.

Growing up, I experienced the political and social changes in that country, like the introduction of universal healthcare when I was around 18 or 19. My schooling was also there, where I attended gymnasium and later taught math and science before immigrating.

explicit_text: My father was a member of the conservative party in Sweden, which he humorously said was as conservative as George McGovern. This comparison always made me chuckle because conservatives in Sweden were not very conservative by American standards. It was a way to understand the political spectrum in my childhood home. So, George McGovern's name came up as a reference point in our family discussions about politics.

explicit_text: Tage Erlander was the Prime Minister of Sweden during my childhood, and my father admired him greatly. Although my father was conservative, he respected Erlander and the Social Democrats for their work, especially in areas like universal healthcare. Towards the end of his life, my father even voted for the Social Democrats, showing how influential Erlander's leadership was in our family discussions.

explicit_text: The Social Democrats played a significant role in shaping Sweden during my youth, especially with policies like universal healthcare. My mother was very supportive of them and their progressive views, while my father, initially conservative, grew to appreciate their work and voted for them later in life. These political dynamics were a common topic in my family and influenced my understanding of social welfare and politics in Sweden.

explicit_text: During the Finnish Winter War, Finland sent many children to Sweden for safety, and my family took in one boy named Esko. He was around 11 or 12 years old, and his father was also a plant breeder like my father. This arrangement was part of a national policy, and we even received a document from the Finnish President thanking us. This experience created a lasting connection between our families, with exchanges of visits and correspondence over the years.

This arrangement was part of a national policy, and we even received a document from the Finnish President thanking us. explicit_text: I have lived in Seattle for many years, and it has been a significant place in my life. I have been involved in the Seattle Labor Chorus and various community activities here. Seattle became my home after immigrating from Sweden, and it shaped much of my adult life, including my work and activism. The city's vibrant community and cultural life have been important to me.
